patient.chief_complaint: worsening fatigue, unsteady gait, and a recent fall

records.content: 78 y/o female with RA x 15 years. On methotrexate 15mg weekly + folic acid + prednisone 10mg daily (unable to taper below 10mg without flare). DAS28: 3.8 (moderate disease activity). Morning stiffness 45 min. Bilateral hand swelling. Assessment: RA, moderately active on current therapy. Plan: Continue methotrexate 15mg weekly. Folic acid 1mg daily. Continue prednisone 10mg (discuss steroid-sparing agents). CBC and LFTs every 2-3 months for methotrexate monitoring. Note: Patient has been on prednisone ≥10mg daily for over 3 years — significant osteoporosis risk. DEXA scan should be repeated.

78 y/o female with chronic LBP from lumbar degenerative disc disease and facet arthropathy. On gabapentin 300mg TID — moderate relief. Reports pain 5/10 on average. Also takes acetaminophen PRN. Assessment: Chronic LBP, partially controlled. Plan: Continue gabapentin. PT referral. Consider increasing gabapentin to 400mg TID if needed.

78 y/o female. Insomnia x 5 years. On zolpidem 5mg at bedtime. Reports 5-6 hours sleep. Concerned about recent near-fall getting up at night (nocturia). Assessment: Insomnia managed with zolpidem. NOTE: Zolpidem is a Beers Criteria medication — inappropriate in elderly (>65) due to increased risk of falls, fractures, and cognitive impairment. Combined with gabapentin (also sedating), this patient has significant CNS depression risk. Plan: Discussed risks with patient. She refuses to stop zolpidem — says she 'can't sleep without it.' Will continue with lowest dose. Safety counseling: nightlight, clear path to bathroom, non-slip mat.

78 y/o female. Multiple chronic conditions. Hemoglobin trending down (11.8 → 11.6 → 11.4). Patient on methotrexate (can cause bone marrow suppression) and chronic prednisone (can cause GI bleeding). Also on omeprazole long-term (reduces iron absorption). Assessment: Mild worsening anemia — need workup if continues. Plan: Continue monitoring. If Hgb drops below 11, iron studies and B12.

78 y/o female for annual exam. Reports increased fatigue. Walking more slowly. Near-falls x 2 in past 3 months (getting up at night). Takes zolpidem + gabapentin — both sedating. Weight 138 lbs, height 5'3" (lost 0.5 inch from last year — height loss). Vitals: BP 142/78, HR 68, BMI 24.4. Assessment: Fatigue — multifactorial (anemia, RA inflammation, medications). Falls risk HIGH (age 78, sedating medications, nocturia, anemia). Plan: Routine labs. Fall risk assessment. DEXA scan — last one was 2018, overdue.

78 y/o female. New findings: 1) HbA1c 5.9% — prediabetic. Likely steroid-induced (on prednisone 10mg daily x 3+ years). 2) Vitamin D 28 — insufficient despite supplementation. 3) Fasting glucose 108 — elevated. 4) Anemia continuing (Hgb 11.2). Assessment: Steroid-induced prediabetes, vitamin D insufficiency, chronic anemia. Plan: Increase vitamin D to 4000 IU daily. Dietary counseling for prediabetes. DEXA scan ordered. Iron studies and B12 for anemia workup.

records.summary: Anemia workup results — anemia of chronic disease
records.content: 78 y/o female. Iron studies essentially normal (ferritin 45, TIBC normal). B12 low normal (280). MCV 94 (normocytic). Reticulocyte count normal. Likely anemia of chronic disease from RA inflammation (ESR/CRP elevated) + possible methotrexate contribution. Assessment: Anemia of chronic disease. Plan: Continue monitoring. Optimize RA control to reduce inflammation. B12 borderline — consider supplementation.

78 y/o female. DEXA results: Lumbar spine T-score: -3.1 (was -2.6 in 2018 — WORSENED). Hip T-score: -2.8 (was -2.3 in 2018 — WORSENED). SIGNIFICANT BONE LOSS despite alendronate 70mg weekly + calcium + vitamin D. Patient is on chronic prednisone 10mg daily x 3+ years — MAJOR contributor to bone loss. Prednisone accelerates osteoporosis and increases fracture risk 5-fold. Assessment: Progressive osteoporosis DESPITE treatment, driven by chronic glucocorticoid use. HIGH FRACTURE RISK. Plan: Continue alendronate. Increase vitamin D. Reinforce fall prevention. MUST address prednisone — need steroid-sparing strategy for RA. Discuss with rheumatology.

79 y/o female with RA. DEXA shows worsening osteoporosis (T-score -3.1 spine). On prednisone 10mg x 3+ years. Methotrexate alone not controlling disease. Need steroid-sparing biologic. Assessment: RA with glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. Plan: Start adalimumab 40mg SQ every 2 weeks (biologic DMARD). Goal: taper prednisone over 3-6 months. Before starting: TB screen, hepatitis B/C screen, ensure vaccinations current. Continue methotrexate as combination therapy.

79 y/o female. On adalimumab x 6 weeks. Joint swelling improved. Morning stiffness reduced to 20 min (was 45). DAS28: 3.2 (mild-moderate). No injection site reactions. No infections. Assessment: Good response to adalimumab. Plan: Begin prednisone taper: reduce to 7.5mg daily x 4 weeks, then 5mg x 4 weeks, then reassess.

79 y/o female. Prednisone reduced to 7.5mg. RA symptoms stable on adalimumab. Feeling slightly better — less fatigued. Still on zolpidem and gabapentin. Reported another near-fall last week — tripped on rug at 3 AM going to bathroom. Assessment: HIGH fall risk continues. On prednisone (weakens bones), zolpidem + gabapentin (impair balance), osteoporosis (T-score -3.1), anemia (fatigue). A fall could be catastrophic. Plan: Reiterated fall prevention. Remove throw rugs. Nightlight. Consider OT home safety evaluation.

79 y/o female. Prednisone reduced from 10mg → 7.5mg → now 5mg. RA well-controlled on adalimumab + methotrexate. DAS28: 2.8 (low disease activity). Assessment: Excellent response. Goal: taper to 2.5mg then off. Plan: Reduce to 2.5mg in 4 weeks. Continue adalimumab and methotrexate.

79 y/o female. Prednisone reduced to 2.5mg. RA stable. No flare. Assessment: Successful taper. Plan: Continue 2.5mg x 4 weeks, then try discontinuing. Adrenal insufficiency risk after long-term steroids — may need slow final taper.

79 y/o female. Attempted to stop prednisone. Developed fatigue, myalgias, mild joint stiffness after 5 days off. Likely adrenal insufficiency from long-term steroid use (>3 years). Restarted prednisone 2.5mg. Assessment: Adrenal suppression preventing complete discontinuation. Plan: Continue prednisone 2.5mg. Consider morning cortisol or ACTH stimulation test. Very slow taper: alternate day dosing, then 1mg, etc.

79 y/o female. Prednisone reduced to 2.5mg (from 10mg). RA improved on adalimumab. Still on zolpidem and gabapentin. Weight 136 lbs. Height 5'2.5" (lost another 0.5 inch — cumulative 1 inch height loss, suggestive of vertebral compression fractures). Vitals: BP 138/76, HR 66, BMI 24.5. Falls risk assessment: HIGH — Timed Up and Go test 18 seconds (>12 = fall risk). Assessment: HIGH fall risk patient. Height loss concerning for occult vertebral fractures. Plan: Spine X-ray for vertebral assessment. Labs. Fall prevention OT referral. MUST address sedating medications.

79 y/o female. Lateral thoracolumbar spine X-ray: mild compression deformity T12 vertebra — consistent with osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture. L2-L4 degenerative changes. Assessment: VERTEBRAL COMPRESSION FRACTURE (T12) — likely from chronic steroid use + osteoporosis. Patient was asymptomatic (incidental finding). This confirms severe osteoporosis with fragility fracture. Plan: Continue alendronate. Referral to endocrinology for osteoporosis optimization. Consider switching to denosumab or teriparatide for more potent anti-fracture therapy. Back brace if symptomatic.

79 y/o female with severe osteoporosis (T-score -3.1 spine), vertebral compression fracture at T12, chronic prednisone use (now 2.5mg, was 10mg x 3+ years). On alendronate x 4 years. Assessment: Osteoporosis treatment failure on alendronate, likely due to chronic glucocorticoid use overwhelming bisphosphonate benefit. Plan: Switch alendronate to teriparatide (anabolic — builds bone, superior to bisphosphonates in glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis). Continue calcium + vitamin D. Monitor with DEXA in 1-2 years.

records.indication: severe osteoporosis with fragility fracture, glucocorticoid-induced

records.content: 79 y/o female. OT home assessment completed. Recommendations: remove 3 throw rugs, install grab bars in bathroom, nightlight in hallway and bedroom, non-slip bath mat, raised toilet seat. Regarding medications: OT noted patient on TWO sedating medications at night (zolpidem + gabapentin evening dose). Strong recommendation to address. Assessment: Environmental modifications ordered. Medication concern persistent. Plan: Implemented home modifications. Will revisit medication discussion with patient.

80 y/o female. RA improved on adalimumab, prednisone down to 2.5mg. Teriparatide started for osteoporosis. Still on zolpidem + gabapentin. Reports 1 fall in past 3 months (Nov 2021) — slipped in bathroom, caught herself, no injury. Weight 134 lbs. Height 5'2.5". Assessment: Stable but fall risk remains HIGH. Plan: Labs. Strongly advised reducing sedating meds.

80 y/o female. RA in low disease activity on adalimumab + methotrexate + prednisone 2.5mg. DAS28: 2.4. Morning stiffness < 15 min. Assessment: Excellent RA control. Plan: Continue current regimen. Attempt prednisone taper to 1mg in 2 months.

80 y/o female. Prednisone now 1mg daily. RA controlled. Back pain stable on gabapentin. Sleep on zolpidem. No recent falls. Reports mild unsteadiness when walking — especially turning corners. Using handrails more. Assessment: Unsteady gait — likely multifactorial: medications (gabapentin + zolpidem residual effect), age, mild anemia, deconditioning. Plan: Physical therapy for balance and gait training. Consider reducing gabapentin evening dose.

80 y/o female for annual exam. SIGNIFICANT: Patient fell last week — tripped over doorstep at night going to bathroom. Landed on left hip. No fracture (X-ray done in ER, negative). Bruised left hip and left wrist. ER visit (outside hospital) — treated and released. Patient still taking zolpidem 5mg at bedtime. Assessment: FALL WITH INJURY in high-risk patient. This is exactly the scenario we have been warning about. She has osteoporosis with T-score -3.1 and vertebral fracture. Sedating medications (zolpidem + gabapentin) are major contributors. Plan: URGENT: Must address zolpidem. Patient again reluctant but agreed to try melatonin 3mg instead. Will taper zolpidem: 5mg → 2.5mg (cut tablet) x 2 weeks → melatonin only. Gabapentin: reduce evening dose from 300mg to 200mg.

80 y/o female. Zolpidem tapered to 2.5mg. Sleeping 4-5 hours (was 5-6). Using melatonin 3mg. Reports difficulty falling asleep but no further falls. Gabapentin evening dose reduced to 200mg. Assessment: Sleep disrupted during taper — expected. Plan: Continue 2.5mg x 1 more week then discontinue. Melatonin + sleep hygiene.

80 y/o female. Zolpidem discontinued. Now on melatonin 3mg only. Sleep: 4-5 hours, fragmented but manageable. No falls since September. Gait steadier per patient and family. Assessment: Improvement in fall risk with zolpidem removal. Plan: Continue melatonin. Sleep hygiene counseling. Gabapentin evening dose remains 200mg.

81 y/o female. Improved since zolpidem removal — no falls x 4 months. Gait steadier. RA controlled. Prednisone 1mg. On teriparatide x 15 months. Weight 133 lbs. Height 5'2" (another 0.5 inch lost). Assessment: Overall improvement in fall risk. Height loss ongoing — may have additional compression fractures. Plan: Routine labs. Consider repeat spine imaging.

records.summary: Lab review — B12 now deficient, magnesium low
records.content: 81 y/o female. B12 195 — now below normal (declining: 280 → 260 → 245 → 218 → 195 over 3 years). On omeprazole long-term and methotrexate — BOTH deplete B12. Also magnesium 1.5 — low (omeprazole-related). Assessment: 1) B12 deficiency — likely from long-term omeprazole + methotrexate. Contributing to her chronic anemia and possibly her unsteady gait (B12 deficiency causes neuropathy and balance problems). 2) Hypomagnesemia from long-term PPI. Plan: Start B12 1000mcg daily (or monthly IM injection). Start magnesium oxide 400mg daily. Consider PPI step-down trial.

81 y/o female. RA in low disease activity. DAS28: 2.2. On adalimumab + methotrexate + prednisone 1mg. Assessment: RA well-controlled. Plan: Attempt to discontinue prednisone 1mg. Alternate days for 2 weeks, then stop. Continue adrenal monitoring.

81 y/o female. Successfully discontinued prednisone after 4+ year course (10mg → 7.5 → 5 → 2.5 → 1 → off). RA remains in low disease activity on adalimumab + methotrexate. No flare. Assessment: Successful steroid discontinuation. Plan: Continue biologics. Monitor for adrenal insufficiency symptoms during stress/illness.

81 y/o female. B12 normalized on supplementation (420). Hemoglobin improving (11.8). Magnesium improving. Gait steadier — no falls x 10 months. Prednisone off. Assessment: Multiple improvements. Plan: Continue B12 and magnesium supplementation. Consider DEXA scan to assess teriparatide effect.

81 y/o female. On teriparatide 22 months (max 2 years). DEXA: Lumbar spine T-score -2.7 (was -3.1 — improved!). Hip T-score -2.5 (was -2.8 — improved!). Assessment: Good response to teriparatide. Plan: Stop teriparatide at 2-year mark (Oct 2023). Transition to denosumab 60mg SQ every 6 months to maintain gains.

81 y/o female. Best she's been in years. Prednisone off, RA controlled, osteoporosis improving, B12 corrected, no falls x 1 year. Weight 134 lbs. Height 5'2" (stable). Assessment: Excellent overall trajectory. Plan: Labs. Continue current regimen.

82 y/o female for annual exam. Doing well. No falls x 16 months. RA controlled. Osteoporosis improving on denosumab. Anemia resolved (Hgb 12.2). B12 corrected. Prednisone off x 8 months. On gabapentin 300mg AM + 300mg noon + 200mg evening for back pain. Melatonin 3mg for sleep. Assessment: Markedly improved overall. Plan: Labs. Continue current regimen.

82 y/o female. Second denosumab injection today. No side effects from first dose. Calcium and vitamin D continued. No new fractures. Height stable. Assessment: Osteoporosis maintenance therapy on track. Plan: Next dose October 2024. CRITICAL: Do NOT miss denosumab doses — rebound bone loss and vertebral fractures can occur if discontinued or delayed.
82 y/o female. Stable. No new complaints. Back pain managed. RA controlled. No falls. Assessment: Doing well. Plan: Continue all medications.

82 y/o female. Comprehensive medication review. Current list: methotrexate 15mg weekly, folic acid 1mg daily, adalimumab 40mg Q2W, prednisone OFF, denosumab Q6M, lisinopril 10mg, omeprazole 20mg, gabapentin 300/300/200mg, melatonin 3mg, calcium 600mg BID, vitamin D 4000 IU, B12 1000mcg, magnesium 400mg. 13 medications. Assessment: Still on long-term omeprazole despite PPI concerns being raised multiple times. GERD symptoms: patient reports only occasional mild heartburn. Plan: Trial PPI step-down — switch to famotidine 20mg BID (H2 blocker). If GERD controlled, continue famotidine and stop omeprazole.

82 y/o female. Switched omeprazole to famotidine 20mg BID x 4 weeks. GERD symptoms controlled — only 1 episode mild heartburn (took antacid). Assessment: Successful PPI discontinuation. Plan: Continue famotidine 20mg BID. This removes the long-term PPI risks (B12 depletion, magnesium depletion, iron malabsorption, fracture risk).

82 y/o female. Best year in recent memory. No falls x 2 years. RA controlled without steroids. Osteoporosis improving. GERD managed on famotidine. Back pain stable. Weight 134 lbs. Height 5'2". Assessment: Excellent. Plan: Labs.
82 y/o female. Third denosumab dose today. Tolerating well. Assessment: Osteoporosis maintenance. Plan: Next dose April 2025.
82 y/o female. All conditions stable. No new issues. Assessment: Excellent management. Plan: Continue current regimen.
83 y/o female. Another stable year. No falls. RA controlled. Assessment: Stable. Plan: Labs.
83 y/o female. Denosumab dose 4 today. Tolerating well. DEXA scheduled for later this year. Assessment: Osteoporosis maintenance. Plan: Next dose October 2025.
83 y/o female. Stable. Back pain managed on gabapentin (reduced dose). No falls. Assessment: Doing well. Plan: Continue all medications.

83 y/o female. DEXA: Lumbar spine T-score -2.4 (was -3.1 in 2020, -2.7 in 2023 — continuing improvement). Hip T-score -2.2 (was -2.8 in 2020, -2.5 in 2023). Assessment: Excellent bone density improvement after teriparatide + denosumab. Off prednisone has helped. Plan: Continue denosumab. DO NOT discontinue without transition plan.

83 y/o female for annual exam. Excellent year. RA controlled. Osteoporosis improving. No falls x 3 years. Weight 135 lbs. Height 5'2". Assessment: Remarkable trajectory for a complex elderly patient. Plan: Labs.

84 y/o female for annual exam. Still doing well overall. RA controlled on adalimumab + methotrexate. Osteoporosis improving on denosumab. No prednisone x 2.5 years. No falls x 3+ years. Current medications: methotrexate, folic acid, adalimumab, denosumab, lisinopril, famotidine, gabapentin, melatonin, calcium, vitamin D, B12, magnesium. 12 medications. Assessment: Complex elderly patient now well-managed. Key lessons from this case: the dangers of long-term steroids, sedating medications in elderly, and the importance of proactive monitoring.

ground_truth_issues.description: Zolpidem (Beers Criteria inappropriate medication in elderly) combined with gabapentin (also sedating) created dangerous CNS depression and fall risk in a patient with severe osteoporosis (T-score -3.1) and vertebral compression fracture. Despite multiple near-falls and documented concerns (records #5, #8, #20, #32, #37), zolpidem was not discontinued until after an actual fall with injury (record #40, September 2022) — over 2 years after the fall risk was first identified. The combination of sedating medications in a frail elderly woman with osteoporosis was predictably dangerous.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria: zolpidem should be avoided in patients >65 due to increased risk of falls, fractures, ER visits, and hospitalizations. Risk is compounded when combined with other CNS depressants (gabapentin). In a patient with severe osteoporosis, any fall carries high fracture risk.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: Zolpidem should have been discontinued or substituted when fall risk was first identified (record #5, May 2020). The combination with gabapentin in a patient with T-score -3.1 was particularly dangerous. Alternatives: melatonin (eventually used), CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia), trazodone at low dose, or sleep hygiene alone.

ground_truth_issues.description: Vitamin B12 progressively declined over 3 years (280 → 260 → 245 → 218 → 195, records #11 through #46) while the patient was on TWO medications that deplete B12: long-term omeprazole (reduces gastric acid needed for B12 absorption) and methotrexate (interferes with folate/B12 metabolism). Despite being flagged as 'low normal' on multiple checks, supplementation was not started until B12 dropped below normal (195 in Jan 2023). The B12 deficiency likely contributed to the chronic anemia, fatigue, and unsteady gait that were attributed to other causes for years.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: Long-term PPI use (>2 years) is associated with B12 deficiency. Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase and can contribute to B12 utilization problems. Guidelines recommend monitoring B12 in patients on long-term PPIs, especially elderly patients with anemia.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: When B12 was first noted as 'low normal' at 280 (record #11, Oct 2020) in a patient on TWO B12-depleting medications, supplementation should have been started proactively rather than waiting 2+ years for it to become frankly deficient.

ground_truth_issues.description: Long-term prednisone (≥10mg daily for 3+ years) was continued despite worsening osteoporosis (T-score declining from -2.6 to -3.1 with vertebral compression fracture) and the development of steroid-induced prediabetes (HbA1c 5.9%). The rheumatology notes from Jan 2020 (record #1) acknowledged steroid-sparing agents were needed, but a biologic was not started until Jan 2021 (record #17) — a full year delay during which bone density continued to worsen. The prednisone dose was not fully tapered until May 2023 — over 3 years after the issue was identified.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: ACR guidelines recommend steroid-sparing strategies for all RA patients on chronic glucocorticoids. Chronic prednisone ≥7.5mg daily significantly increases fracture risk, and glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is the most common form of secondary osteoporosis. DEXA should be repeated every 1-2 years in patients on chronic steroids.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: When DEXA in Nov 2020 (record #13) showed worsening osteoporosis despite treatment, the steroid-sparing biologic should have been started immediately rather than waiting 2 more months. Additionally, the initial acknowledgment of the need for steroid-sparing agents (record #1, Jan 2020) should have triggered action within weeks, not months.
